---
title: "Glim -- Project Documentation"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: true
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: default
---

**Last Updated:** 2026-03-25

**Purpose:** This document is the cumulative, archival record of the Glim project. It contains all design context, technical decisions, feature documentation, and development history. This is the single source of truth for how Glim works, why it works that way, and where it's going.

---

# 1. Project Vision & Philosophy

## 1.1 What is Glim?

Glim is a personalized interactive companion that lives in a browser tab (desktop) and as a PWA on mobile. It takes the form of a glowy owl-cat-moth spirit - a round, chubby creature with owl eyes, spread wing-arms, bobble antennae, a curly tail, and fawn-like dot markings. Glim floats against an atmospheric twilight sky with twinkling stars, shooting stars, nebula wisps, silhouette mountains, pine trees, and ambient glowing bugs.

Glim is evolving into a personalized wellness and productivity hub with a character-driven interface. Beyond its original role as a passive companion with wellness reminders and journaling, Glim will encompass water tracking, nutrition logging, step and exercise tracking, mood tracking, to-do lists, habit tracking, a pomodoro timer, goal/streak tracking, historical trends, and more. The creature isn't decoration - it's the personality layer that makes tracking daily habits feel like sharing your day with a friend rather than logging data into a clinical app. All data syncs across devices via Firebase so the desktop and mobile experiences stay in lockstep.

It delivers personalized messages that reference the user's real life - their thesis project, their cats, their hobbies, their values - so it feels like a friend rather than a generic motivational tool.

The project began in early March 2026 as "Blorb," a bioluminescent blob desktop companion. It evolved through several creature designs (Blorb, Mochi, Sprig) before settling on the owl-cat-moth spirit form. The name "Glim" was chosen because it evokes both "glimmer" and "glimpse."

## 1.2 Design Philosophy

The following values guide all design decisions for Glim:

**Personality over polish.** Glim should feel alive and idiosyncratic, not like a polished product. Quirks, failed fly attempts, existential musings about being made of pixels - these are features, not bugs.

**Passive presence.** On desktop, Glim sits in a pinned tab and doesn't demand attention. It offers gentle reminders and observations. The user glances over when they want to, not because Glim forces it.

**Radical personalization.** Messages reference the user's actual life - specific cats by name, specific thesis topics, specific values and hobbies. Generic motivational content is avoided in favor of messages that could only be for this person.

**Science-backed self-care.** Wellness messages are grounded in actual neuroscience and research (BDNF, gratitude neuroscience, sleep science, hydration research). Glim is a nerdy friend who drops real facts, not platitudes.

**Show don't tell.** Glim's personality emerges through what it says and how it behaves, not through labels or descriptions. The user discovers behaviors (shake detection, off-screen wandering, bug chasing) through interaction.

**Absurdism and warmth.** The tone balances genuine care with playful absurdity. Glim is simultaneously deeply supportive and hilariously self-aware about being a fictional owl-cat-moth made of SVG paths.

---

# 2. Technical Design

## 2.1 Technology Stack

### Current Stack (pre-migration)

| Component | Technology | Why |
|---|---|---|
| UI Framework | React 18 (CDN) | State management for complex interactive behaviors; hooks for timers, effects, and refs |
| JSX Transpilation | Babel Standalone 7.23.9 (CDN) | Allows JSX syntax in-browser without a build step; keeps the code human-readable |
| Utility CSS | Tailwind CSS (CDN) | Used lightly for layout classes (`flex`, `absolute`, `rounded-full`) |
| Rendering | SVG (inline) | The creature, background landscape, and ambient elements are all pure SVG with CSS animations |
| State Persistence | `window.storage` API (artifact) / `localStorage` polyfill (standalone HTML) | Journal entries, poke count, and settings survive across sessions |

**CDN Dependencies (required on first load, cached afterward):**

- `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/react/18.2.0/umd/react.production.min.js`
- `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/react-dom/18.2.0/umd/react-dom.production.min.js`
- `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/babel-standalone/7.23.9/babel.min.js`
- `https://cdn.tailwindcss.com`

**Known console warnings (not errors):**

- Tailwind CDN produces a "should not be used in production" warning. This is expected for a local HTML file and does not affect functionality.
- Babel produces an "in-browser transformer" warning. Also expected and harmless.

### Planned Stack (post-migration, decided 2026-03-25)

| Layer | Technology | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Build tool | Vite | Industry standard. Handles JSX at build time (eliminates Babel CDN). Dev server with hot module replacement. Optimized production builds |
| UI Framework | React 18 (npm) | Migrating from CDN to Vite-managed npm package. Same framework, proper dependency management |
| State management | Zustand | Tiny (1KB) shared store library. Replaces 30+ useState calls in DesktopPet with a centralized store accessible from any component |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS (build version) | Replaces CDN version. Build tool scans code and includes only used classes. Eliminates console warning. Productive for building many UI panels with consistent spacing and responsive behavior |
| Data visualization | Chart.js | Framework-agnostic chart library. Handles line charts (trends), bar charts (daily breakdowns), progress rings (goals). Well-documented |
| Backend/sync | Firebase (Firestore + Auth) | Cross-device sync, authentication, real-time listeners. Generous free tier. Build after trackers exist |
| PWA | vite-plugin-pwa | Auto-generates service worker, manifest, caching strategies. Eliminates manual sw.js authoring |
| Hosting | GitHub Pages (initially), possibly Firebase Hosting later | Free, auto-deploys from repo |
| Rendering | SVG (inline) | Unchanged - creature and background remain pure SVG with CSS animations |

**What gets eliminated by migration:** Babel Standalone CDN (~800KB loaded on every page open), Tailwind CDN (replaced by build version), in-browser JSX compilation (build time instead), ad-hoc HTML export pipeline (replaced by `npm run build`)

**What gets added:** Node.js + npm (local development requirement), `package.json` (dependency manifest), `vite.config.js` (build configuration). All setup handled by Claude Code.

**Migration decision rationale:** See Decision Register entry 2026-03-25 "Adopt Vite build system." Alternatives evaluated: staying on CDN-only (rejected - doesn't scale to 25+ features), Svelte/SvelteKit (rejected - React's larger ecosystem and skill transferability outweigh Svelte's simpler model for this project).

### System Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the four-layer architecture of future Glim and how the layers interact.

![Glim system architecture](figures/glim_system_architecture.png)

**Layer 1 - User-facing (top).** Two parallel systems the user interacts with directly. The **Creature World** (left) is everything visual and behavioral: the OwlMoth SVG, the twilight background, autonomous behaviors (sleep, fly, chase), and all interactions (click, drag, shake, purr). The **Feature Panels** (right) are the productivity and wellness tools: daily trackers (water, steps, mood, sleep), productivity tools (todos, pomodoro, quick captures), reflection tools (journal, habits, gratitude), and analysis views (trends, charts, streaks).

**Layer 2 - Personality Engine (middle, amber).** The bridge between the creature and the trackers. This is what makes Glim feel alive rather than being a cute creature sitting next to a boring tracking app. It contains the **message pools** (775+ personalized messages across 29 categories), the **reaction system** (the centralized `glimReact()` handler that picks the right animation and message when any tracker event fires), and the **reminder timers** (wellness, move, eye break). The creature sends behavior states down to the personality engine; the personality engine sends animations and messages back up. The feature panels send events down (water logged, task completed); the personality engine sends nudges and celebrations back up.

**Layer 3 - Data Layer (blue).** Stores everything and handles cross-device sync. **Zustand stores** hold the current state in memory (15 domains, fast reads/writes for the UI). **localStorage** persists data on-device (survives page refresh). **Firebase** syncs data to the cloud (shows up on your other devices). Data flows left to right: Zustand → localStorage → Firebase.

**Layer 4 - Platform Foundation (bottom, gray).** The build tools and libraries everything runs on: Vite (build system), React (UI framework), Tailwind (CSS), Chart.js (data visualization), and PWA support (offline capability).

## 2.2 File Structure & Build Process

**Current files:**

| File | Purpose | Location |
|---|---|---|
| `desktop-pet.jsx` | React artifact version (source of truth for development) | Claude artifact / outputs |
| `glim.html` | Standalone HTML export (self-contained, runs in any browser) | outputs / local machine |
| `glim-messages.txt` | Extracted inventory of all 775+ messages by category | outputs |
| `eye-comparison.jsx` | Eye-closing style comparison artifact (reference only) | outputs |
| `sky-mockup-v2.jsx` | Perpetual twilight sky background mockup (reference only) | outputs |

**HTML Export Build Process:**

The standalone HTML file is built by wrapping the JSX source with a header (React/Babel/Tailwind CDN links, `window.storage` polyfill) and footer (closing tags). The process:

1. Strip the `import` line from `desktop-pet.jsx`
2. Replace `export default function DesktopPet` with `function DesktopPet`
3. Append a `ReactDOM.createRoot` render call
4. Concatenate: HTML header + transformed JSX body + HTML footer

```bash
tail -n +2 desktop-pet.jsx | sed 's/^export default function DesktopPet/function DesktopPet/' > /tmp/glim-body.jsx
echo '' >> /tmp/glim-body.jsx
echo '    ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")).render(<DesktopPet />);' >> /tmp/glim-body.jsx
cat /tmp/glim-header.html /tmp/glim-body.jsx /tmp/glim-footer.html > glim.html
```

The header file includes a `window.storage` polyfill that maps the Claude artifact storage API to `localStorage`, so the same storage calls work in both environments.

**[FOLLOW-UP NEEDED]:** The header and footer temp files (`/tmp/glim-header.html`, `/tmp/glim-footer.html`) are not persisted and must be recreated each session. These should be stored as permanent files in the repo once GitHub Pages is set up.

## 2.3 Codebase Organization

The `desktop-pet.jsx` file (~2460 lines) is organized in the following order:

1. **Import** - React hooks
2. **Utility functions** - `pickRandom()`, `getTimeOfDay()`, `getColors()`
3. **Message pools** (~lines 1-700) - All `const` arrays: `MESSAGES` object (time-of-day, clicked, rapidClick, shaken, startled, purr, flyFail, mindfulClick, sleepTalk, idle, wellness, encouragement), plus standalone arrays (`MINDFULNESS`, `MOVE_REMINDERS`, `EYES_REMINDERS`, `MOVE_DONE_RESPONSES`, `EYES_DONE_RESPONSES`, `DISCOVERIES`, `JOURNAL_PROMPTS`, `JOURNAL_NUDGES`, `SCIENCE_FACTS`)
4. **Color system** - `getTimeOfDay()` and `getColors()` functions mapping hours to hue/saturation values
5. **Background component** - Sky crossfade layers, stars, shooting stars, nebula wisps, landscape SVG, floating motes, horizon glow, moon
6. **AmbientBugs component** - 5 glowing bugs with unique flight paths
7. **SpeechBubble component** - Message display with frosted glass styling
8. **PersistentReminder component** - Wellness reminder display with dismiss buttons
9. **OwlMoth component** - The creature SVG: body, wings, face disc, eyes (with squish-to-line closing), beak, blush, antennae, tail, feet, body dots
10. **DesktopPet component** (main) - All state, refs, effects, timers, interaction handlers, and the main render tree

**Key state management patterns:**

- `useRef` for values that need to be current inside async callbacks (timers, intervals) without causing re-renders. Critical refs: `isSleepingRef`, `isPurringRef`, `lastInteractionRef`, `dragStartRef`, `justDraggedRef`, `dragVelocityRef`, `wanderDriftRef`, `settingsInitRef`
- `useCallback` for stable function references passed as dependencies
- `useState` for values that need to trigger re-renders (visual state, message display, UI panels)

**Stale closure pattern:** All `useEffect` hooks that run asynchronous callbacks (timers, intervals) use `.current` refs instead of state variables for checks that need to reflect the latest value. This was the source of multiple bugs where Glim would talk in its sleep or ignore sleep state, because `specialAnim` was captured at effect creation time rather than checked at callback execution time.

## 2.4 Persistent Storage

| Key | Data Format | Purpose | Survives Code Updates |
|---|---|---|---|
| `glim-journal` | JSON array of `{id, text, prompt, date}` objects | Journal entries | Yes |
| `glim-pokes` | String (integer) | Lifetime poke count | Yes |
| `glim-settings` | JSON object `{wellnessInterval, moveInterval, eyesInterval}` | User-configured reminder intervals | Yes |

**Storage backend:**

- In the Claude artifact: `window.storage` API (Anthropic's persistent key-value store, shared across artifact versions)
- In standalone HTML: `localStorage` polyfill wrapping `localStorage.getItem/setItem/removeItem`

**Browser behavior for local files:**

- Chrome: treats all `file://` pages as same origin. Storage persists as long as the file is opened in Chrome, even if the file path changes.
- Firefox: stricter origin policies for `file://`. Storage may not persist if the file is moved to a different path.
- Safari: may restrict `localStorage` for local files entirely.

**Recommendation:** Always overwrite the same file path when updating the standalone HTML to avoid origin issues.

---

# 3. Platform Specifications

## 3.1 Shared Features

Both desktop and mobile versions share the same codebase and the following features:

- Full creature with all visual animations (float, bounce, puff, purr rock, fly attempt, chase, secret spin)
- Eye tracking (cursor position), squish-to-line eye closing (sleep, purr, rare blink)
- All mouse interaction types: click, double-click, long press (purr), drag, shake detection
- **[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** Touch equivalents of the above interactions do not exist yet
- All message pools and delivery system (messages persist until next message replaces them)
- Time-of-day sky crossfade system (5 perpetual twilight backgrounds, 2-minute transitions)
- Background with stars (brightness varies by time), shooting stars, nebula wisps, pine tree landscape, ambient bugs, floating motes, moon (late night only)
- Journal with prompts, persistent storage, past entry viewer
- Wellness reminders (configurable intervals for general wellness, move, and eye breaks)
- Proactive timer reset buttons ("just moved", "eyes rested")
- Settings panel with interval sliders (persisted across sessions)
- Persistent poke count
- All autonomous behaviors (idle chatter, sleep, fly attempt, bug chase, discoveries, wandering, secret spin)
- **[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** Off-screen adventure behavior

## 3.2 Desktop Version

**Form factor:** Pinned browser tab. User opens `glim.html` (local file) or visits the GitHub Pages URL and pins the tab.

**Primary role:** Passive companion. Glim sits visible on screen while the user works, offering ambient encouragement, wellness reminders, and occasional surprises. The user glances at Glim between tasks.

**Desktop-specific considerations:**

- Mouse-based interaction (hover states on buttons, cursor-following eye tracking)
- Larger creature size (360x390px)
- Floating panels (settings, journal) positioned top-right
- "Click to say hi" prompt visible below creature name

## 3.3 Mobile Version (PWA)

**Form factor:** Progressive Web App installed via "Add to Home Screen." Full-screen, no browser chrome, own app icon.

**Primary role:** Active wellness tool. The mobile version is intended to expand beyond the passive companion role into a more interactive personal tool.

**Planned mobile-specific features:**

- Water intake tracking
- More in-depth journaling features
- To-do list tracking

**Mobile adaptations:**

**[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** The following mobile adaptations are planned but do not exist in the current codebase as of 2026-03-24:

- Touch event handlers parallel to all mouse events (unified pointer system via `getPointerXY()` helper)
- Responsive layout: `isMobile` flag (screen width < 600px) scales creature, glow ring, and panels
- Larger tap targets (44px minimum per Apple guidelines) for gear/journal buttons on mobile
- `touch-action: none` to prevent browser default gestures during drag
- Settings and journal panels expand to near-full-width on small screens
- Off-screen return listens for `touchstart` in addition to `mousemove`

**[FOLLOW-UP NEEDED]:** PWA files (manifest.json, service worker, app icons) have not been created yet. GitHub Pages repo has not been set up.

## 3.4 One Codebase Strategy

The current approach is a single file that adapts to both platforms using responsive detection:

**[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** The `isMobile` flag and responsive detection do not exist in the current codebase. The planned implementation:

```javascript
const [winWidth, setWinWidth] = useState(window.innerWidth);
const isMobile = winWidth < 600;
```

**Current state:** All event handlers are mouse-only (`onMouseDown`, `mousemove`, `mouseup`). No touch events are attached. No responsive sizing logic exists. The creature is hardcoded at 360x390px.

**Planned strategy:** Both mouse and touch event listeners would be attached simultaneously. Browsers ignore whichever doesn't apply. Layout differences would be handled via the `isMobile` flag (creature size, panel widths, button sizes).

**Open question:** As mobile-specific features are added (water tracking, to-do lists), the codebase will contain code that only runs on one platform. The current plan is to use `isMobile` as a feature flag to show/hide mobile-only UI. If the divergence becomes too large, the alternative is separate entry points that share a common component library. This decision should be revisited once 2-3 mobile-specific features have been implemented.

**[FOLLOW-UP NEEDED]:** Decide on the threshold for splitting into separate entry points vs. continuing with feature flags.

---

# 4. Feature Inventory

## 4.1 Creature Design & Visual System

**Creature:** Owl-cat-moth spirit rendered as inline SVG within a 220x240 viewBox, displayed at 360x390px on desktop (responsive on mobile).

**Visual elements:**

- Radial gradient body (green-teal-blue-purple-pink, shifts with time of day via hue rotation)
- Spread wing-arms with translucent fill
- Round face disc with lighter tint
- Large circular eyes with pupil tracking (pupils follow cursor/touch position, normalized offset)
- Squish-to-line eye closing: eyes are ellipses where `ry` transitions from 12 to 1.5 for sleep, purr, and rare blink. Pupils and highlight reflections hidden when eyes closed.
- Small triangular beak
- Circular blush marks
- Bobble antennae on curved stalks (perk outward when speech bubble appears)
- Curly tail drawn as a bezier path
- Small feet ellipses
- Scattered dot markings (like a fawn)
- Outer glow via CSS `drop-shadow` filter, intensity varies with state (brighter when puffed/purring)

**Color system:** Hue rotates through the day: green (150) at early morning, teal (175) morning, blue (220) afternoon, purple (270) evening, pink (310) late night. Saturation is fixed at 65%. All creature colors derive from these base values.

**Animations:**

- `owlFloat`: Gentle 4s vertical bob (default idle)
- `owlBounce`: Happy bounce triggered by encouragement and journal saves
- `purrRock`: 1.2s side-to-side rocking during long press (rotate +/-5deg)
- `flyAttempt`: Upward movement then drop (creature-level CSS animation)
- `chaseBug`: Lateral movement toward target bug
- `secretSpin`: Rare 2s glowy spin (3% chance per behavior cycle)
- Body transform states: squeezed (on click), puffed (startled), happy (enlarged eyes + bounce)
- Random wing twitches (independent, every 6-12s)
- Rare blink (every 45-90s, 250ms squish, skipped during sleep/purr)

## 4.2 Interaction System

All interactions currently use mouse events only. **[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** Touch/pointer unification (planned `getPointerXY()` helper) does not exist in the codebase yet.

| Interaction | Trigger | Response |
|---|---|---|
| Click/tap | Single click on Glim | Random clicked message; wakes from sleep with startled puff; context-aware during mindfulness |
| Double-click/tap | Double click on Glim | Fly attempt (fails adorably with screen-level animation) |
| Long press | Hold 500ms without moving | Purring with rocking animation and closed eyes; message from purr pool |
| Drag | Move 10px+ after mousedown/touchstart | Glim follows pointer; window-level handlers ensure drag continues outside creature bounds |
| Shake | 3+ direction reversals in 500ms during drag | Shaken message with 2s cooldown |
| Rapid click | 4+ clicks within 2s | Overwhelmed/flustered response |
| Startled | First click after 5+ min idle (50% chance) | Puff-up animation with startled message |

**Drag return:** When released, Glim drifts back to center with a 3s ease-out CSS transition. `requestAnimationFrame` double-buffering ensures the transition CSS applies before the position changes. `isReturning` flag prevents other interactions during the drift. `justDraggedRef` blocks clicks for 300ms after release.

## 4.3 Behavior System

Autonomous behaviors are scheduled via a recursive `setTimeout` loop with random delays (90-210 seconds between behavior checks). All behaviors check `isSleepingRef.current` before firing. Behavior selection uses a single weighted random draw so documented percentages are exact.

| Behavior | Chance | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sleep | 3% (after 15 min idle) | Until clicked | Eyes close, Zzz animation, only sleepTalk messages shown. Very rare by design. If idle threshold not met, this slot becomes "nothing happens." |
| Fly attempt | 20% | 1.6s | Screen-level upward movement then drop; fly-fail message on land |
| Bug chase | 25% | 3s | Targets specific ambient bug; pre-chase, catch (60%), or miss (40%) dialog |
| Discovery | 30% | instant | Glim finds something in its world (20 discovery messages) |
| Secret spin | 3% | 2.2s | Unexplained glowy spin; no explanation given |
| Off-screen wander | **[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** | -- | Planned: Glim drifts off-screen and returns on pointer activity. Not in codebase. |
| Nothing | 19% | -- | No behavior fires this cycle |
| Idle wandering | Every 15-35s | 5.5s | Small random drift (40px) then back; cancelled if drag starts |
| Idle chatter | Every 15-40s | Persistent | Cycles through message pools: encouragement (16%), mindfulness (16%), journal nudge (5%), science fact (8%), idle (20%), time-of-day (35%) |

## 4.4 Background & Environment

**Sky crossfade system:** Five sky gradient layers rendered simultaneously as stacked divs. Only the layer matching the current time period has opacity 1; the rest are at opacity 0. All layers have `transition: opacity 120s ease` for a 2-minute crossfade between time periods.

| Time Period | Hours | Sky Character | Star Brightness | Moon |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Early Morning | 6am-9am | Navy to warm muted horizon | 60% | No |
| Morning | 9am-12pm | Navy to cool teal-green | 35% | No |
| Afternoon | 12pm-5pm | Navy to blue-slate, brighter horizon | 25% | No |
| Evening | 5pm-9pm | Deep purple to burnt orange horizon | 50% | No |
| Late Night | 9pm-6am | Near black | 90% | Yes |

**Design principle:** Always moody, always twilight, always stars visible. No sun. The sky shifts in hue and warmth but never reaches full daylight.

**Other background elements:**

- 80 twinkling stars with glow halos on largest ones
- Shooting stars every 12-37s
- 3 nebula wisps with drift animations (opacity varies with hue)
- Horizon glow (color and intensity varies by time period, 120s transition)
- Silhouette landscape: 2 mountain layers
- Pine tree silhouettes: 4 clustered left, 4 clustered right, 4 scattered (varying sizes and opacity for depth)
- Ground plane
- 5 ambient bugs with unique flight paths (chaseable)
- 12 floating motes near Glim

## 4.5 Message System Overview

Glim has 775+ messages across 29 categories. Messages persist on screen until the next message replaces them (no auto-hide timer).

**Message delivery:** The `showMessage` function sets the message text, shows the speech bubble, and accepts optional `wellness` and `msgType` parameters. There is no auto-hide timer; messages stay visible until the next one fires.

**Message personalization themes:** Thesis project (Proteomic Signatures of Ketamine-Treated Cortical Astrocytes, Dr. Blanco-Suarez), cats (Spooky's specific behaviors - flopping, trilling, fish plushie, cardboard tower, feather toy, bed sheets; Pepita's behaviors - loud purring, following Spooky, getting braver), partner (Alex), siblings (Lados, Anora), improv (Artificial Idiocy), sobriety, values (absurdism, radical inclusion, gifting, decommodification, self-expression, participation, immediacy), teaching (TA for genetics).

**Full message inventory:** See `glim-messages.txt` for all messages organized by pool.

## 4.6 Journal System

**Access:** Pen icon (&#9997;) next to settings gear, top-right.

**Write view:**

- Random journaling prompt displayed in tinted box (39 prompts, short-entry focused)
- Shuffle button for new prompt
- Text area with focus-highlighting matching current hue
- Character count and save button (activates only when text present)
- Glim reacts on save with happy bounce + save message

**Past entries view:**

- Scrollable list, newest first
- Each entry shows date/time, prompt used (italic), and full text
- Delete button per entry (turns red on hover)

**Storage:** Entries saved to `glim-journal` key as JSON array. Each entry: `{id: timestamp, text, prompt, date: ISO string}`.

**Journal prompts (39):** Organized into mood/feelings, gratitude/joy, reflection, looking forward, self-check-in, curiosity/play, connection, absurdist/fun, and open categories. Designed for one-sentence to short-paragraph entries.

**Journal nudges:** ~5% of idle chatter is a gentle journal reminder (8 messages).

## 4.7 Wellness Reminders

Three configurable reminder timers with jitter to prevent predictability:

| Reminder | Default Interval | Jitter | Delivery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wellness (general) | 20 min | 30% | Message in speech bubble |
| Move | 45 min | None | Persistent reminder below creature |
| Eye break | 20 min | None | Persistent reminder below creature |

**Proactive reset buttons:** "just moved" and "eyes rested" buttons below the poke counter. Clicking one wakes Glim (if sleeping), clears any active reminder, restarts the timer at the full configured interval, and triggers an acknowledgment message.

**Settings panel:** Gear icon, top-right. Frosted glass panel with sliders for each reminder interval.

## 4.8 Settings Panel

**Access:** Gear icon (&#9881;) top-right. Mutually exclusive with journal panel (opening one closes the other).

**Controls:**

- Wellness reminders: 10-60 min slider (default 20)
- Move reminders: 15-90 min slider (default 45)
- Eye break reminders: 10-60 min slider (default 20)

Slider accent colors match Glim's current hue. All slider values are persisted to the `glim-settings` storage key and restored on page load.

---

# 5. Message Inventory

## 5.1 Pool Structure & Categories

Messages are stored in `const` arrays at the top of `desktop-pet.jsx`. The main `MESSAGES` object contains keyed pools, and additional standalone arrays exist for specialized categories.

**Full message list:** See `glim-messages.txt` for the complete inventory organized by pool with numbering.

## 5.2 Message Counts by Category

| Category | Count | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Early Morning | 16 | Time-of-day idle chatter (6am-9am) |
| Morning | 20 | Time-of-day idle chatter (9am-12pm) |
| Afternoon | 30 | Time-of-day idle chatter (12pm-5pm) |
| Evening | 27 | Time-of-day idle chatter (5pm-9pm) |
| Late Night | 28 | Time-of-day idle chatter (9pm-6am) |
| Clicked | 64 | Single click/tap on Glim |
| Rapid Click | 22 | 4+ clicks in 2 seconds |
| Shaken | 18 | Shaking Glim during drag |
| Startled | 15 | First click after 5+ min idle |
| Purr | 22 | Long press |
| Fly Fail | 16 | After fly attempt animation |
| Mindful Click | 12 | Clicking during a mindfulness message |
| Sleep Talk | 16 | Idle chatter while sleeping |
| Idle | 109 | General idle chatter (personality, musings, cat references) |
| Wellness | 31 | Wellness timer |
| Encouragement | 75 | Idle chatter with happy bounce |
| Move Reminders | 14 | Move timer |
| Eyes Reminders | 10 | Eye break timer |
| Move Done | 6 | Dismissing move reminder |
| Eyes Done | 5 | Dismissing eyes reminder |
| Mindfulness | 119 | Idle chatter (values, calm, trust, it's-okay-to-not-know) |
| Discoveries | 20 | Autonomous discovery behavior |
| Journal Prompts | 39 | Journal write view |
| Journal Nudges | 8 | Idle chatter (~5% chance) |
| Off-screen Return | **[NOT IMPLEMENTED]** | Planned for off-screen adventure behavior |
| Science Facts | 35 | Idle chatter (~8% chance) |
| Bug Chase (pre/catch/miss) | 19 | Autonomous bug chase behavior |
| Drag Release | 9 | After being dragged and released |
| Journal Save | 7 | After saving a journal entry |
| **Total** | **~775** | (excludes 20 planned off-screen return messages) |

## 5.3 Content Themes

**Personal references:** Spooky (flop, trill, fish plushie, cardboard tower, feather toy, bed sheets, greeting at door), Pepita (loud purr, following Spooky, getting braver, cuddling), Alex (silly, talkative), siblings (Lados, Anora - big sister references), Artificial Idiocy improv team.

**Research:** Thesis title ("Proteomic Signatures of Ketamine-Treated Cortical Astrocytes"), Dr. Blanco-Suarez, multi-omics, astrocytes, proteomics workflow, R/Python, thesis encouragement.

**Values:** Absurdism, radical inclusion, gifting, decommodification, radical self-expression, participation, immediacy, chasing fun/joy/curiosity. Drawn from Burning Man principles but presented as personal values.

**Self-care science (35 facts):** BDNF and exercise (8), gratitude neuroscience (7), sleep science (4), hydration and cognition (3), social connection (4), nature exposure (2), breathing and mindfulness (3), miscellaneous (4). Each prefixed with a casual tag ("science fact:", "nerd fact:", "did you know:", etc.).

**Emotional support:** Trust yourself, things will work out, it's okay to not know, everyone feels stupid sometimes, calm is a skill, imposter syndrome. ~30 messages across mindfulness and encouragement pools.

**Thesis-specific encouragement:** 15 messages rooting for thesis completion, referencing the specific project, advisor, and the significance of the work.

**Humor:** Science jokes scattered across time-of-day pools, Glim's existential musings about being made of pixels, self-aware commentary on its own behaviors.

---

# 6. Deployment & Distribution

## 6.1 Standalone HTML (Desktop)

**Setup for the user:**

1. Download `glim.html`
2. Save to a stable file path (same path every time for localStorage persistence)
3. Double-click to open in browser
4. Pin the tab

**Requirements:** Modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) with internet connection on first load (CDN caching). Works offline after first load.

## 6.2 GitHub Pages PWA (Mobile)

**Status:** Not yet implemented.

**Planned setup:**

1. Create GitHub repo with `index.html`, `manifest.json`, `sw.js`, app icons
2. Enable GitHub Pages in repo settings
3. URL: `username.github.io/glim`
4. On phone: visit URL in Safari/Chrome, "Add to Home Screen"
5. Glim appears as installable app with own icon, full screen, no browser chrome

**Files needed:**

- `index.html` - the single Glim file (same as `glim.html` but served from HTTPS)
- `manifest.json` - PWA configuration (name, icons, display mode, theme colors)
- `sw.js` - Service worker for offline caching
- `icon-192.png` and `icon-512.png` - App icons (could be generated from Glim SVG)

**[FOLLOW-UP NEEDED]:** Create PWA files, set up GitHub repo, test on iOS and Android.

## 6.3 Update Workflow

**Current workflow (desktop only):**

1. Edit `desktop-pet.jsx` in Claude artifact
2. Test changes in artifact preview
3. Run the HTML export build process (tail/sed/cat pipeline)
4. Download `glim.html` and save to same file path (overwrite previous version)
5. Refresh the browser tab
6. Journal entries and poke count persist (stored in localStorage, not in the code)

**Planned workflow (both platforms):**

1. Edit `desktop-pet.jsx` in Claude
2. Test in artifact
3. Export to HTML
4. Push to GitHub repo
5. GitHub Pages auto-deploys
6. Desktop: download new HTML (or visit the URL)
7. Mobile: PWA auto-updates from GitHub Pages on next open

---

# 7. Known Limitations & Bugs

**Stale closure pattern:** Multiple bugs arose from `useEffect` callbacks capturing state variables at creation time rather than execution time. Resolved by introducing `isSleepingRef` and `isPurringRef` that stay current via sync effects. Any new timer-based features should follow this pattern.

**CSS gradient transitions:** CSS cannot natively transition between `linear-gradient` values. Solved with an opacity crossfade approach (5 stacked layers, only the active one visible). The 2-minute transition is smooth but adds 5 DOM elements permanently.

**Drag return snap:** Early implementations had a visible snap at the end of the drag-return animation caused by the CSS `transition` property changing when `isReturning` cleared. Solved by using a constant transition (`isDragging ? "none" : "3s smooth"`) that never changes during the return.

**localStorage origin for local files:** Browser behavior varies for `file://` origins. Chrome is permissive (same origin for all local files). Firefox and Safari may be stricter. Mitigated by recommending users always save to the same file path.

**Tailwind dependency:** Tailwind CSS is loaded from CDN but barely used (a handful of utility classes, primarily in PersistentReminder). Could be replaced with inline styles to eliminate the dependency and the console warning.

**Babel in-browser transpilation:** Adds a small load time on first open. The alternative (pre-compiling to `React.createElement` calls) would make the code unreadable and unmaintainable for a single-file project.

**No data export for journal:** If localStorage is cleared or the browser changes, journal entries are lost. A JSON export/import feature has been discussed but not implemented.

**No touch event support:** All interaction handlers are mouse-only (`onMouseDown`, `mousemove`, `mouseup`). Glim is non-functional on touch-only devices. This is the primary blocker for mobile deployment.

**No responsive layout:** Creature size is hardcoded at 360x390px. No `isMobile` flag, no responsive sizing. UI panels are fixed-width and may overflow on small screens.

**Off-screen adventure not implemented:** The behavior is documented in the roadmap and was planned during the 03-23/24 session but was never coded. No `isOffScreenRef`, no `OFFSCREEN_RETURN` message array, no off-screen detection logic exists.

### 7.1 Resolved Issues (2026-03-24 audit)

**Timer memory leaks (fixed):** `scheduleBlink`, `scheduleTwitch`, and the shooting star scheduler all used recursive `setTimeout` without cleanup refs. The blink effect was particularly problematic because its `[isPurring]` dependency array meant every purr cycle spawned a new parallel blink chain. All three now use local timer refs with proper cleanup returns.

**Wander drift orphan (fixed):** The idle wandering effect cleaned up `wanderTimerRef` but not `wanderDriftRef` on teardown. If the effect re-ran mid-drift, the drift-back timeout was orphaned. Now both refs are cleared.

**Behavior probability drift (fixed):** The autonomous behavior system used cascading independent `Math.random()` draws, causing effective probabilities to drift from documented values (e.g., discovery was documented as 30% but effective rate was ~17.5%). Replaced with a single weighted random draw against a 0-100 roll. Sleep probability corrected from 10% to 3%. Bug chase catch rate corrected from 50/50 to 60/40.

**Eye tracking callback churn (fixed):** `handleMouseMove` depended on `specialAnim` state, causing the window event listener to be removed and re-added on every animation state change. Switched to `isSleepingRef.current` check, making the callback stable with an empty dependency array.

**Dead code removed:** `calcDuration` function and unused `durationOverride` parameter in `showMessage` were removed. The function was defined but never called; the parameter was accepted but ignored.

**Settings not persisted (fixed):** Wellness, move, and eye break interval sliders now save to `glim-settings` storage key and restore on load. Uses a `settingsInitRef` guard to prevent the save effect from overwriting storage with defaults before the load effect runs.

---

# 8. Roadmap & Future Features

## 8.1 Product Vision (updated 2026-03-25)

Glim is evolving from a passive desktop companion into a personalized wellness and productivity hub. The creature isn't decoration - it's the personality layer that makes tracking water intake feel like talking to a friend rather than logging data into an app. The goal is to consolidate all personal management and self-care tools into a single application with a character-driven interface.

**Core differentiator:** Every tracking app on the market is clinical and impersonal. Glim is warm, funny, personalized, and opinionated about your wellbeing. The creature reacts to your progress, notices patterns, celebrates streaks, and nudges you when you're slipping - all in its own voice, with references to your actual life.

**Platform strategy:** Single codebase, cross-device sync via Firebase. Desktop (pinned browser tab) serves as passive companion during work. Mobile (PWA) serves as active wellness tool throughout the day. Both share all data in real time.

## 8.2 Infrastructure (build before features)

These items are prerequisite to or should be built alongside the feature work below.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Touch event support | Not started | **High** | Primary mobile blocker. Needs `getPointerXY()` helper, dual mouse/touch handlers |
| Responsive layout | Not started | **High** | `isMobile` flag, responsive creature sizing, panel widths, 44px+ tap targets |
| Vite migration | Not started | **High** | Migrate from CDN-loaded React/Babel/Tailwind to Vite-based build system. Enables multiple source files, proper imports, optimized builds, dev server with hot reload. Eliminates Babel CDN. See Section 2.1 Planned Stack |
| Zustand state management | Not started | **High** | Migrate 30+ useState calls from DesktopPet into Zustand shared stores. Organize by domain (creature, trackers, UI, settings). Prerequisite for multi-component architecture |
| File separation | Not started | **High** | Split single 2460-line file into: message data, creature component, background component, UI panels, stores, reaction system. Enabled by Vite migration |
| Sync-ready data model | Not started | **High** | Design storage schema for all trackers before building them. Event-based logging (not running totals) for merge-safe sync. See Section 8.8 |
| Glim reaction system | Not started | **High** | Centralized system for creature responses to tracker events: `glimReact('water_logged', { current: 6, goal: 8 })`. Keeps personality layer separate from tracker code |
| Behavior state machine | Not started | **Medium** | Formal states and transitions for creature behavior. Prevents impossible state combinations as feature count grows |
| PWA setup (manifest, service worker, icons) | Not started | **Medium** | Required for mobile deployment. Blocked by GitHub repo setup |
| GitHub repository and Pages deployment | Not started | **Medium** | Repo structure, .gitignore, README, GitHub Pages config |
| Cross-device sync (Firebase) | Not started | **Later** | Firestore database, authentication, offline-first with background sync. Build after trackers exist and data model is proven |
| Off-screen adventure behavior | Not started | **Low** | Creature behavior: drift off-screen, return on pointer activity. Needs `isOffScreenRef`, `OFFSCREEN_RETURN` messages |
| Remove Tailwind dependency | Not started | **Low** | Replace utility classes with inline styles. Eliminates CDN dependency and console warning |

## 8.3 Daily Tracking

Features that log daily data toward goals. These form the core of the mobile experience.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water tracking | Not started | **High** | Log glasses/oz toward a configurable daily goal. Glim reacts to each log, celebrates hitting the goal, notices patterns over time. Data model: timestamped log events (not running totals) for sync-safe merging |
| Step tracking | Not started | **Medium** | Log daily steps toward a goal. Could integrate with phone pedometer APIs if available, or manual entry. Glim cheers milestones |
| Nutrition / food logging | Not started | **Medium** | Log meals with optional macros/calories. More complex data model - meals contain foods, foods have nutritional data. Scope needs narrowing before build (full macro tracking vs. simple meal logging) |
| Exercise tracking | Not started | **Medium** | Log workouts - type, duration, notes. Connects to move reminders. Glim acknowledges specific activities |
| Sleep tracking | Not started | **Medium** | Log bedtime and wake time. Connects to existing late-night messages. Glim notices patterns ("you've been staying up past midnight three days in a row") |
| Mood tracking | Not started | **Medium** | Daily mood check-in (emoji or scale). Glim responds to mood, notices trends, connects mood to other tracked data over time |
| Medication / supplement reminders | Not started | **Low** | Timed reminders for specific items with quick "done" buttons. Similar to existing wellness reminders but user-configurable per item |

## 8.4 Productivity

Features for task management and focus.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| To-do lists | Not started | **High** | Tasks with completion tracking. Glim reacts to checking things off. Needs: add, complete, delete, reorder. Persistent across sessions |
| Pomodoro timer | Not started | **Medium** | 25-min work sessions with break announcements. Glim counts down, announces breaks, tracks completed sessions per day. Integrates with existing wellness reminder system |
| Quick capture / brain dump | Not started | **Low** | Fast text box for stray thoughts, timestamped and saved. Different from journaling - no prompt, no reflection, just capture. Glim can reference past captures in idle chatter |

## 8.5 Reflection and Wellbeing

Features for self-awareness and personal growth.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Enhanced journaling | Not started | **High** | Expand beyond current prompt-and-write system. Could include: guided reflection templates, tagging, search, longer entries, image attachment (mobile camera). Scope needs narrowing |
| Habit tracker | Not started | **Medium** | Generic repeating habits beyond the specific trackers (e.g., "floss," "practice guitar," "read 20 pages"). Daily check-off with streak tracking |
| Gratitude log | Not started | **Medium** | One line per day, "one thing you're grateful for." Separate from journal. Dedicated tracker with streak counting. Connects to existing gratitude science facts |
| Social check-in tracker | Not started | **Low** | Lightweight "who did you connect with today?" log. Glim notices if you haven't reached out to anyone in several days. Not a contact manager - just awareness |
| Weekly review prompt | Not started | **Low** | Once a week, Glim nudges a structured reflection: what went well, what was hard, what do you want next week to look like. More structured than daily journaling |

## 8.6 Trends and Summaries

Features that analyze and present tracked data over time.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goal setting and streak tracking | Not started | **High** | Unified goal system across all trackers. Daily/weekly goals with streak counting. Glim celebrates streaks, encourages recovery from broken ones |
| Daily/weekly summaries | Not started | **Medium** | Glim generates a progress report: "today you drank 7 glasses of water, completed 4 tasks, walked 8200 steps, and journaled." Could display as a dedicated summary panel or as Glim's idle chatter |
| Historical trends visualization | Not started | **Medium** | Charts/graphs showing tracked data over days, weeks, months. Water intake over time, mood trends, exercise frequency. Mobile-friendly data visualization |
| Custom countdowns | Not started | **Low** | Things to look forward to. "Thesis defense: 47 days." "Artificial Idiocy show: 3 days." Glim references these in idle chatter |

## 8.7 Gifting and Customization

Features for sharing Glim with others.

| Feature | Status | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glim Builder | Not started | **Low** | Web tool where you fill in someone's name, pet names, interests, values, etc. and generate a customized Glim. Outputs a personalized HTML file or account setup |
| Gifting workflow | Not started | **Low** | Package a customized Glim for someone. Needs Glim Builder + delivery mechanism (download, email, link) |
| Editable message panel | Not started | **Low** | Let recipients of gifted Glims modify/add their own messages without touching code |

## 8.8 Data Model Specification (designed 2026-03-25)

### Foundational Design Decisions

**Storage architecture: One key per domain.** Each tracker gets its own storage key (`glim-water`, `glim-todos`, etc.) rather than one monolithic object. Rationale: writes are smaller and faster, a failed write only affects one domain, maps cleanly to Firebase documents/subcollections, maps cleanly to Zustand stores, and loading the journal doesn't require loading months of nutrition data.

**ID generation: UUIDs.** Every event uses `crypto.randomUUID()`. Guaranteed unique across devices, eliminates collision concerns during sync. Available in all modern browsers.

**Date handling: Local dates derived from UTC timestamps.** Every event stores a full UTC timestamp in `createdAt` and a derived local-date string in `date` (format: `"2026-03-25"`). The timestamp is the truth; the date field is a convenience for grouping by day. Local date derived via `new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-CA')`.

**Soft delete: All domains.** Deleting an entry sets `deletedAt` to a timestamp rather than removing it from the array. Required for sync (prevents deleted entries from being resurrected by an unsynced device). Enables undo for free. Entries with `deletedAt` older than 30 days can be permanently purged.

**Sync-safe data principle:** All tracker data stored as timestamped event logs, not running totals. Derived values (daily totals, streaks, averages) are computed from events, never stored.

### How Data Flows Through Glim

The diagram below traces a concrete example - logging a glass of water - through all four layers of Glim's architecture. This same pattern applies to every tracker action (completing a task, logging a meal, checking off a habit, etc.).

![Data flow example: logging water](figures/glim_data_flow_example.png)

**The left column (teal/blue)** is the data path - what any tracking app does: save data, update the UI. **The right column (amber/purple)** is the personality path - what only Glim does: interpret every data event through the reaction system and make the creature respond. The two columns are wired together through `glimReact()`, which fires on every tracker event.

The bottom section (steps 10-11) shows the cross-device sync payoff: you log water on your phone at lunch, Firebase pushes the event to your desktop in the background, and desktop Glim mentions your progress in its idle chatter. Both devices share the same Glim "brain."

This flow pattern is universal. Only the specific message pool, animation, and event name change between trackers. That's why the reaction system is centralized: one system handles personality for all 15 domains.

### Storage Key Map

| Storage Key | Type | Sync Strategy | Grows? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `glim-settings` | Config object | Last-write-wins by `lastModified` | No |
| `glim-pokes` | Counter with delta tracking | Additive deltas | No |
| `glim-journal` | Event log (entries array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-water` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-todos` | Event log (items array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-steps` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-exercise` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-mood` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Slowly |
| `glim-sleep` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Slowly |
| `glim-nutrition` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-habits` | Definitions + event log | Merge both arrays by ID | Yes |
| `glim-gratitude` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Slowly |
| `glim-captures` | Event log (entries array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Yes |
| `glim-countdowns` | Item list | Merge + dedupe by ID | Barely |
| `glim-social` | Event log (logs array) | Merge + dedupe by ID | Slowly |

### Domain Schemas

#### Settings (`glim-settings`)

No events, no history. Last-write-wins on sync.

```javascript
{
  wellnessInterval: 20,                         // Minutes
  moveInterval: 45,
  eyesInterval: 20,
  goals: {
    water: { amount: 8, unit: "glass" },        // "glass" | "oz" | "ml"
    steps: { amount: 10000 },
    sleep: { targetHours: 8 },
  },
  dayBoundary: "midnight",                      // Future: configurable hour
  lastModified: "2026-03-25T14:30:00.000Z",
}
```

**Migration from current format:** Current `glim-settings` stores `{ wellnessInterval, moveInterval, eyesInterval }`. On first load with new schema, wrap existing values and add `goals`, `dayBoundary`, `lastModified`.

#### Pokes (`glim-pokes`)

```javascript
{
  total: 847,
  lastSyncedTotal: 840,
  lastModified: "2026-03-25T14:30:00.000Z",
}
```

**Sync strategy:** Compute delta per device: `delta = total - lastSyncedTotal`. Add both deltas to the synced total. Reset `lastSyncedTotal` on both devices.

**Migration:** Current format is a plain string integer. On first load, parse to `{ total: parseInt(value), lastSyncedTotal: 0, lastModified: now }`.

#### Journal (`glim-journal`)

```javascript
{
  entries: [
    {
      id: "a1b2c3d4-...",                      // UUID
      text: "today felt like a slow-motion nature documentary",
      prompt: "describe your day as if narrating a nature documentary",
      tags: [],                                 // Future: user-applied tags
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T21:30:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

**Migration:** Current format is a plain array with `{ id: timestamp_int, text, prompt, date: ISO_string }`. Transform each: generate UUID, rename `date` to `createdAt`, derive `date` from `createdAt` in local time, add `deletedAt: null`, add `tags: []`.

**Glim reactions:** `journal_saved`, `journal_streak` (3+ consecutive days).

#### Water (`glim-water`)

The model tracker that sets the pattern for all daily trackers.

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "e5f6g7h8-...",
      amount: 1,                                // Number of units
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T10:30:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

Goal lives in `glim-settings.goals.water`, not duplicated here.

**Derived values (computed, never stored):** Today's total (`logs.filter(date === today && !deletedAt).reduce(sum, amount)`), progress percentage, current streak (consecutive days meeting goal), best streak, weekly average.

**Glim reactions:** `water_logged` ("nice! that's glass 5 of 8"), `water_goal_met`, `water_streak` (3/7/14/30 days), `water_nudge` (no water by configurable time).

#### Todos (`glim-todos`)

```javascript
{
  items: [
    {
      id: "m3n4o5p6-...",
      text: "finish thesis methods section",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T09:00:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      completedAt: null,                        // Null = not done, timestamp = done
      deletedAt: null,
      sortOrder: 0,                             // For manual reordering
    },
  ],
}
```

`completedAt` is a timestamp (not boolean) to record when the task was finished. Feeds into daily summaries, productivity trend analysis, and undo (clear timestamp to un-complete).

**Derived values:** Active count, completed today, completion rate.

**Glim reactions:** `task_completed` ("checked off! [n] down, [m] to go"), `all_tasks_done`, `task_created`.

#### Steps (`glim-steps`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "u1v2w3x4-...",
      count: 3200,
      source: "manual",                         // "manual" | future: "healthkit" | "google_fit"
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T18:00:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

`source` field future-proofs for automatic pedometer imports. Initial version is manual entry only.

**Derived values:** Today's total, goal progress, streak, weekly/monthly averages.

**Glim reactions:** `steps_logged`, `steps_goal_met`, `steps_streak`.

#### Exercise (`glim-exercise`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "y5z6a7b8-...",
      type: "walk",                             // Free text or from recentTypes
      durationMinutes: 30,
      notes: "walked around campus between classes",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T12:30:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
  recentTypes: ["walk", "yoga", "dance", "improv warm-up"],
}
```

`recentTypes` auto-populated from past `type` values for quick-select UI buttons.

**Derived values:** Workouts this week, total duration this week, most common type, streak (days with at least one exercise).

**Glim reactions:** `exercise_logged` (resets move timer), `exercise_streak`, type-specific commentary ("improv warm-up counts as exercise and i will die on this hill").

#### Mood (`glim-mood`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "c9d0e1f2-...",
      value: 4,                                 // 1-5 scale (UI displays as emojis)
      note: "good day, got a lot done",         // Optional
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T21:00:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

Numeric 1-5 scale enables trend analysis and cross-tracker correlation. UI maps numbers to emojis. One entry per day convention: second entry for same date soft-deletes the first.

**Derived values:** Today's mood, 7-day average, 30-day trend direction, correlation hints (mood vs sleep, mood vs exercise).

**Glim reactions:** `mood_logged` (empathetic for low, celebratory for high), `mood_improving` (upward trend over a week).

#### Sleep (`glim-sleep`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "g3h4i5j6-...",
      bedtime: "2026-03-24T23:30:00.000Z",
      waketime: "2026-03-25T07:15:00.000Z",
      quality: null,                             // Optional 1-5, logged on wake
      notes: null,
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T07:20:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",                        // Date you WOKE UP
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

`date` is the wake-up date (matches how people think: "I got 7 hours last night" refers to today).

**Derived values:** Hours slept (from bedtime/waketime), 7-day average, trend, bedtime consistency (std dev of bedtimes).

**Glim reactions:** `sleep_logged` (comments on duration), `sleep_improving`, late-night messages become data-aware ("you averaged 6 hours this week...").

#### Nutrition (`glim-nutrition`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "k7l8m9n0-...",
      meal: "lunch",                            // "breakfast" | "lunch" | "dinner" | "snack"
      description: "leftover pasta, side salad", // Free text
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T12:45:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

v1 is simple meal logging (what you ate, free text). No calorie/macro tracking initially. Schema can add optional `calories`, `protein`, `carbs`, `fat` fields later without breaking anything.

**Derived values:** Meals logged today, meals this week, patterns (skipping breakfast?).

**Glim reactions:** `meal_logged`, `meal_skipped` (no breakfast by 11 AM), pattern-aware ("you've logged breakfast every day this week").

#### Habits (`glim-habits`)

```javascript
{
  definitions: [
    {
      id: "o1p2q3r4-...",
      name: "floss",
      frequency: "daily",                       // "daily" | "weekdays" | "weekly"
      createdAt: "2026-03-20T10:00:00.000Z",
      archivedAt: null,                         // Soft archive (stop tracking without losing history)
    },
  ],
  logs: [
    {
      id: "w9x0y1z2-...",
      habitId: "o1p2q3r4-...",                  // References the definition
      date: "2026-03-25",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T22:00:00.000Z",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

Definitions and logs are separate arrays. A definition is created once; logs are created each time the habit is checked off. Archiving a habit hides it from the UI without losing history.

**Derived values:** Per-habit streak, best streak, completion rate (7/30 days), today's status. Overall: habits done today vs total active.

**Glim reactions:** `habit_checked`, `habit_streak`, `habit_all_done` (all daily habits checked).

#### Gratitude (`glim-gratitude`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "a3b4c5d6-...",
      text: "Alex made me laugh so hard at dinner",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T21:30:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

Intentionally minimal. One field, low friction. Power is in consistency (streak) and Glim referencing past entries in idle chatter.

**Glim reactions:** `gratitude_logged`, `gratitude_streak`, occasional idle reference to a random past entry.

#### Quick Capture (`glim-captures`)

```javascript
{
  entries: [
    {
      id: "e7f8g9h0-...",
      text: "look into that BDNF paper Dr. B mentioned",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T14:22:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

Same structure as gratitude but different intent (utilitarian vs reflective) and different Glim personality.

**Glim reactions:** `capture_saved` ("got it. stored."), occasional idle reference to old captures.

#### Countdowns (`glim-countdowns`)

```javascript
{
  items: [
    {
      id: "i1j2k3l4-...",
      title: "Thesis defense",
      targetDate: "2026-05-15",
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T10:00:00.000Z",
      completedAt: null,                        // Auto-set when date passes
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
}
```

**Derived values:** Days remaining (targetDate vs today). Past events auto-complete.

**Glim reactions:** Idle chatter references ("thesis defense in 51 days"), milestone alerts at 30/14/7/3/1 days, celebration on the day.

#### Social Check-ins (`glim-social`)

```javascript
{
  logs: [
    {
      id: "m5n6o7p8-...",
      who: "Alex",                              // Free text
      how: "in person",                         // "in person" | "text" | "call" | "video"
      createdAt: "2026-03-25T19:00:00.000Z",
      date: "2026-03-25",
      deletedAt: null,
    },
  ],
  recentPeople: ["Alex", "Lados", "Anora"],     // Auto-populated for quick selection
}
```

**Derived values:** Days since last check-in, unique people this week, most frequent contacts.

**Glim reactions:** `social_logged`, `social_gap` (gentle nudge after 3+ days without a check-in).

### Data Maintenance

**Soft-delete cleanup:** Periodically purge entries where `deletedAt` is older than 30 days.

**Archive strategy (future):** For high-frequency trackers (water, nutrition), raw logs older than 90 days could be archived to `glim-[domain]-archive`, keeping only computed daily summaries for trend display. Not needed at launch.

**Export/import:** A single "Export All Data" function collects all 15 keys into one JSON object. "Import" writes each key back. Covers backup, device migration, and debugging.

### Migration Path from Current Storage

| Current Key | Current Format | Migration |
|---|---|---|
| `glim-journal` | Plain array `[{ id: int, text, prompt, date: ISO }]` | Wrap in `{ entries: [...] }`, generate UUIDs, rename `date` to `createdAt`, derive local `date`, add `deletedAt: null`, add `tags: []` |
| `glim-pokes` | Plain string integer | Parse to `{ total: int, lastSyncedTotal: 0, lastModified: now }` |
| `glim-settings` | `{ wellnessInterval, moveInterval, eyesInterval }` | Add `goals`, `dayBoundary`, `lastModified` |

Migration runs automatically on first load with new schema. Detects old format by checking structure (e.g., if `glim-journal` parses to an array rather than an object with `entries` key).

## 8.9 Creature Personality Ideas

Not features per se, but ideas for how Glim's personality layer extends as features are added.

- **Keyboard shortcuts:** 'j' for journal, 'p' to pet, spacebar to poke
- **Trophy collection:** Glim accumulates stats (stars caught, bugs eaten, journal entries, goals met) shown as tiny trophies
- **Seasonal events:** Holiday hat, birthday mode, first-poke anniversary
- **Routine detection:** Glim remembers when you usually show up and greets differently if early/late
- **Cross-tracker commentary:** Glim notices correlations in idle chatter: "you slept 8 hours and your mood was great today... coincidence? (it's not, it's science)"
- **Achievement messages:** New message pools for each tracker milestone: first water goal hit, 7-day streak, 100 tasks completed, etc.
- **Personalized nagging:** Glim escalates nudges if a tracker hasn't been updated: gentle day 1, pointed day 2, dramatic day 3

---

# 9. Decision Register

> **Entry template:**
>
> ### [DATE] - [Short Decision Title]
> **Decision:** [What was decided]
> **Rationale:** [Why this choice was made]
> **Alternatives considered:** [What else was on the table and why it was rejected]
> **Impact:** [What this decision affects downstream]

### 2026-03-03 - Creature redesign from Blorb to owl-cat-moth

**Decision:** Abandoned the original blob creature design in favor of an owl-cat-moth spirit with defined features (eyes, wings, antennae, tail).
**Rationale:** The blob was too abstract to convey personality through animation. The owl-cat-moth form allows for expressive eyes, wing movements, and antenna reactions that communicate emotion.
**Alternatives considered:** Moth-axolotl-cat hybrid ("Mochi"), chunky forest creature ("Sprig"). Mochi was too complex; Sprig was too earthy and lost the cute-weird charm.
**Impact:** Defined the entire visual language and animation system.

### 2026-03-24 - Perpetual twilight sky (no sun)

**Decision:** All time-of-day backgrounds maintain a moody, twilight atmosphere. No sun. Stars always visible. Moon only at late night.
**Rationale:** User rejected the full-daylight morning/afternoon mockups as too bright and inconsistent with Glim's aesthetic. The perpetual twilight maintains the atmospheric, cozy feel across all times.
**Alternatives considered:** Realistic day/night cycle with sun positioning. Rejected because full daylight looked wrong with Glim's glowy translucent design.
**Impact:** Background system uses 5 gradient layers with opacity crossfade rather than a single transitioning gradient.

### 2026-03-24 - Squish-to-line eye closing

**Decision:** Eyes close by transitioning the ellipse `ry` from 12 to 1.5, creating a horizontal line. Pupils and highlights hidden when closed.
**Rationale:** User preferred this over curved smile arcs and half-moon crescents. The squish is clean, anime-inspired, and works well for sleep, purr, and blink without extra geometry.
**Alternatives considered:** Curved smile arcs (too dramatic for blinks), half-moon crescents (too complex, previous rectangle attempt looked mechanical).
**Impact:** Eyes are now ellipses rather than circles in all states.

### 2026-03-24 - Messages persist until replaced

**Decision:** Removed auto-hide timer. Messages stay on screen until the next message fires.
**Rationale:** User was missing messages when looking away from the screen. Since idle chatter cycles every 15-40s anyway, old messages don't linger forever.
**Impact:** Removed all hardcoded duration arguments from `showMessage` calls. `calcDuration` function was later removed entirely during the 2026-03-24 audit.

### 2026-03-24 - isSleepingRef pattern for async callbacks

**Decision:** Introduced `isSleepingRef` (and later `isPurringRef`) as refs that stay in sync with state via dedicated effects. All timer callbacks check `.current` instead of captured state.
**Rationale:** Multiple bugs where Glim talked in its sleep were caused by stale closures in `useEffect` callbacks capturing `specialAnim` at effect creation time. The ref pattern ensures callbacks always see the latest value.
**Alternatives considered:** Adding `specialAnim` to every dependency array. Rejected because it causes effects to re-run and reset timers unnecessarily.
**Impact:** Pattern should be followed for any new timer-based feature that needs to check sleep or purr state.

### 2026-03-24 - One codebase for desktop and mobile

**Decision:** Maintain a single file that adapts to both platforms via responsive detection (`isMobile` flag) and dual event handlers (mouse + touch).
**Rationale:** Separate codebases would require maintaining 800+ messages and all behavior logic in two places. The divergence is currently small (layout and tap targets).
**Impact:** Mobile-specific features will use `isMobile` to show/hide UI. Decision should be revisited if platform divergence becomes significant.
**Note (2026-03-24 audit):** Neither `isMobile` nor touch handlers are implemented yet. Decision stands as the planned approach.

### 2026-03-24 - Single weighted random draw for behavior system

**Decision:** Replaced cascading independent `Math.random()` draws in the autonomous behavior scheduler with a single `Math.random() * 100` roll against defined weight buckets.
**Rationale:** The cascading approach caused effective probabilities to drift from documented values. Discovery was documented as 30% but had an effective rate of ~17.5% because each check was conditional on all prior checks failing. The weighted draw guarantees exact percentages.
**Alternatives considered:** Keeping cascading draws and updating documentation with effective rates. Rejected because the code intent is clearer with a single draw that matches the labels, and the "nothing happens" bucket is now explicit (19%) rather than implicit.
**Impact:** Behavior percentages are now: secret spin 3%, sleep 3%, fly 20%, chase 25%, discovery 30%, nothing 19%. Sleep probability corrected from 10% to 3% to match "very rare by design" intent. Bug catch rate corrected from 50/50 to documented 60/40.

### 2026-03-24 - Persist settings intervals to storage

**Decision:** Settings slider values (wellness, move, eye break intervals) are now saved to `glim-settings` storage key and restored on page load.
**Rationale:** Journal entries and poke count were already persisted, but settings reset on every refresh. Users who customize their reminder intervals would lose those changes.
**Alternatives considered:** Leaving settings as session-only. Rejected because the inconsistency with other persisted state was confusing, and the implementation cost was trivial.
**Impact:** New storage key `glim-settings`. Uses `settingsInitRef` guard to prevent save-before-load race condition.

### 2026-03-24 - Timer cleanup pattern for recursive schedulers

**Decision:** All recursive `setTimeout` schedulers now use local timer refs with proper `useEffect` cleanup returns. The blink effect was refactored to use `isPurringRef` instead of `isPurring` state in its dependency array, reducing it to `[]` (mount-only).
**Rationale:** The blink scheduler had an active memory leak: its `[isPurring]` dependency meant each purr cycle spawned a new parallel timer chain. The twitch and shooting star schedulers had the same structural fragility. While not actively leaking (empty deps), they had no cleanup path if the component ever remounted.
**Alternatives considered:** None. This was a correctness fix.
**Impact:** Pattern for all future schedulers: use a local `{ current: null }` timer object, clear it in the cleanup return. Avoid state dependencies in scheduler effects - use refs instead.

### 2026-03-25 - Stay on React (not migrate to vanilla JS)

**Decision:** Continue building Glim on React rather than migrating to vanilla JavaScript.
**Rationale:** The planned feature set (to-do lists, nutrition tracking, water tracking, step tracking, habit tracking, mood tracking, etc.) involves complex data-driven UI with lists, forms, counters, and multiple views of shared state. This is React's core strength. While the creature/timer system pays a "React tax" (refs, sync effects, cleanup returns), the productivity/wellness tracker features that make up the bulk of future development would be significantly harder to build and maintain in vanilla JS. The trade-off favors React given the project's direction.
**Alternatives considered:** (1) Full migration to vanilla JS - rejected because the planned features are exactly what React handles well. (2) Hybrid approach keeping JSX/Babel but dropping React state management - rejected as too novel/unsupported. (3) Stay on React but adopt a state machine - adopted as a complementary improvement (see data model and reaction system decisions).
**Impact:** React, ReactDOM, and Babel remain as dependencies. The ref/cleanup patterns documented in the audit remain the standard for timer-based code. New features should use React's state model for UI and the planned reaction system for creature personality integration.

### 2026-03-25 - Cross-device sync via Firebase (design now, build later)

**Decision:** Glim will use Firebase (Firestore) for cross-device sync. The sync-ready data model will be designed before building any new trackers, but the Firebase integration itself will be built after trackers exist.
**Rationale:** Cross-device sync is essential to the product vision (phone for active tracking throughout the day, desktop for passive companion during work). Designing the data model for sync from the start is cheap. Retrofitting sync onto a data model designed for local-only storage is expensive and error-prone. But building the actual Firebase integration before having features that generate data to sync would be premature.
**Alternatives considered:** (1) Manual JSON export/import - rejected as too cumbersome for daily use across two devices. (2) Build sync first - rejected because we don't know the exact data shapes yet. (3) Different backend (Supabase, custom server) - Firebase chosen for generous free tier, built-in auth, real-time listeners, and offline-first support.
**Impact:** All tracker data models must use timestamped events (not running totals) for merge-safe sync. See Section 8.8 for data model design notes. Firebase SDK will be added as a CDN dependency when sync is implemented.

### 2026-03-25 - Event-based data model for all trackers

**Decision:** All tracker data will be stored as timestamped event logs rather than running totals or current-state snapshots.
**Rationale:** Event logs are trivially mergeable across devices (combine lists, deduplicate by ID). Running totals create conflicts when two devices modify the same value independently. Event logs also enable historical trend analysis, daily/weekly summaries, and cross-tracker correlations without maintaining separate aggregation tables.
**Alternatives considered:** Running totals with conflict resolution heuristics. Rejected because the resolution logic would be different for each tracker type, is error-prone, and loses information (you can derive a total from events but can't derive events from a total).
**Impact:** Every tracker must assign unique IDs and timestamps to each event. Derived values (daily totals, streaks, progress toward goals) are computed from the event log, not stored directly. See Section 8.8 for the planned unified storage structure.

### 2026-03-25 - Centralized Glim reaction system

**Decision:** Glim's personality responses to tracker events will be handled by a centralized reaction system rather than being baked into each feature's code.
**Rationale:** With 10+ planned trackers, each needing personality integration (Glim celebrates, nudges, comments, notices patterns), embedding reaction logic in each tracker would scatter personality code across the entire codebase. A centralized `glimReact(event, data)` function keeps the personality layer as a single maintainable system that all trackers call into.
**Alternatives considered:** Per-tracker reaction code (current journal save pattern). Rejected because it doesn't scale to 10+ features and makes personality consistency hard to maintain.
**Impact:** New design pattern for all features: trackers emit events, the reaction system handles creature responses. The current journal save reaction (inline `showMessage` + `triggerHappy`) should eventually be refactored to use this system.

### 2026-03-25 - Adopt Vite build system

**Decision:** Migrate from in-browser CDN loading (React, Babel, Tailwind CDNs) to a Vite-based build system with npm-managed dependencies.
**Rationale:** The single-file, no-build approach doesn't scale to 25+ features. In-browser Babel compilation (~800KB loaded on every page open) is slow and unnecessary. A build tool enables: multiple source files with proper imports, optimized production builds, dev server with hot reload, proper dependency versioning, PWA auto-generation via vite-plugin-pwa, and Tailwind build-time scanning (eliminates the CDN console warning). The codebase is projected to grow from ~2460 lines to 5000-8000+, which requires file separation.
**Alternatives considered:** (1) Continue with CDN-only approach - rejected because it can't support multiple source files, proper imports, or build-time optimization. (2) Webpack - rejected because Vite is faster, simpler to configure, and the current industry standard for new projects. (3) Parcel - rejected as less mature than Vite for this use case.
**Impact:** Development workflow changes from "edit HTML file, open in browser" to "run `npm run dev`, edit source files, see changes instantly." Deployment changes from "download HTML" to "`npm run build`, push to GitHub Pages." Requires Node.js + npm on dev machine. Claude Code handles setup.

### 2026-03-25 - React over Svelte for UI framework

**Decision:** Stay on React rather than migrating to Svelte or SvelteKit.
**Rationale:** Evaluated both frameworks against Glim's full feature roadmap. Svelte offers a simpler programming model that eliminates the stale closure / ref sync pattern issues in timer-heavy creature code. However, React's ecosystem advantages are decisive for this project: (1) vastly more tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, and example code for every feature Glim needs (Firebase integration, chart libraries, PWA setup), (2) skills transfer to other projects and the broader web development field, (3) Claude has deeper training on React patterns, and (4) existing 2460-line codebase doesn't require rewriting. The timer-side complexity is manageable with documented patterns.
**Alternatives considered:** Svelte / SvelteKit - genuine technical advantages for Glim's creature code (no stale closures, simpler reactive model, smaller bundle), but smaller ecosystem, less transferable skills, and rewrite cost (~1200 lines of logic).
**Impact:** React remains the framework. Zustand adopted for state management (replaces 30+ useState calls with a shared store). All React patterns documented in the audit (ref sync, timer cleanup) remain the standard.

### 2026-03-25 - Zustand for state management

**Decision:** Adopt Zustand as a shared state management library, replacing the current pattern of 30+ `useState` calls in the DesktopPet component with a centralized store.
**Rationale:** As features grow from 1 main component to 15+ tracker panels, passing state through props becomes unworkable. Zustand provides a simple shared store that any component can read from without prop drilling or React Context boilerplate. At 1KB, it adds negligible bundle weight.
**Alternatives considered:** (1) React Context - built-in but verbose and causes unnecessary re-renders in consuming components. (2) Redux - powerful but heavy boilerplate, overkill for this project. (3) Jotai/Recoil - atom-based, good but less intuitive than Zustand's single-store model.
**Impact:** State will be organized into domain stores (creature state, tracker data, UI state, settings). Any component can access any store directly. Existing useState calls will be migrated incrementally as components are extracted.

### 2026-03-25 - One storage key per domain

**Decision:** Each tracker gets its own storage key (`glim-water`, `glim-todos`, etc.) rather than a single monolithic `glim-data` object.
**Rationale:** (1) Smaller, faster writes (only the changed domain is written). (2) Failed writes only affect one domain. (3) Maps cleanly to Firebase documents/subcollections. (4) Maps cleanly to Zustand stores. (5) Loading one feature doesn't require loading all data into memory.
**Alternatives considered:** Single `glim-data` key containing everything. Simpler for export/import (one blob) but every save writes the entire dataset, growing slower as data accumulates. Also, a partial write failure could corrupt everything.
**Impact:** 15 storage keys defined. Export/import collects all keys into one JSON. Firebase sync maps each key to a Firestore document or subcollection.

### 2026-03-25 - Soft delete for all domains

**Decision:** Deleting any entry sets a `deletedAt` timestamp rather than removing it from storage. Items with `deletedAt` are filtered out of UI. Permanent purge after 30 days.
**Rationale:** Hard delete breaks sync. If Device A deletes an entry and Device B hasn't synced, Device B pushes the entry back on next sync. Soft delete ensures both devices see the deletion. Also enables free undo.
**Alternatives considered:** Hard delete with a separate "tombstone" log tracking deleted IDs. More complex, achieves the same result with more code.
**Impact:** Every schema includes a `deletedAt` field. All UI queries must filter on `!deletedAt`. Periodic cleanup job removes entries where `deletedAt` > 30 days.

---

# 10. Project Log

> **Entry template:**
>
> ### [DATE] - [Phase/Activity Label]
> **Work performed:** [What was done]
